title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their experience of working from home. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Home Office: Four People on the Reality of Remote Work

texts.A:
### Laura, 35, Content Writer
[43]I have been working from home for six years and I still feel a small thrill of satisfaction every morning when I start work in my own space without a commute.[/43] The freedom to structure my own day is something I would find very hard to give up. That said, I had to be ruthless about setting boundaries early on – my family assumed that working from home meant I was available for domestic tasks at any time. [47]I now have a dedicated home office with a door that stays shut during work hours, which sounds simple but made an enormous difference to my productivity and my stress levels.[/47] [51]The one thing I genuinely miss about office life is the unplanned corridor conversations that led to my best ideas.[/51]
texts.B:
### Patrick, 43, Project Manager
[44]When my company shifted to fully remote working three years ago, I assumed I would struggle because I had always defined myself as a people person.[/44] I did struggle at first – the silence felt oppressive and I was constantly looking for reasons to call colleagues unnecessarily. But I adapted. [48]I now run all my team meetings on video calls, and I've been surprised to find that some team members who rarely spoke in the office have become the most engaged voices in remote sessions.[/48] [52]The challenge I still find difficult is onboarding new staff remotely; building trust takes far longer when you can't share a physical space.[/52]
texts.C:
### Yuki, 29, UX Designer
[45]I live in a small flat and worked from the kitchen table for two years before I finally accepted that my home setup was making me miserable rather than flexible.[/45] The line between work and rest had completely dissolved, and I was effectively always in the office. I spent a significant amount of money setting up a proper corner of my bedroom as a workspace and the psychological shift was immediate. [49]I've since become almost evangelical about the importance of a dedicated space for remote workers, which I talk about constantly in my professional network.[/49] I've also introduced a strict shut-down ritual at the end of each working day to signal to my brain that work is over.
texts.D:
### Greg, 50, Financial Analyst
[46]I was deeply resistant to remote working when it was first proposed, and I spent the first three months convinced my career would suffer from reduced visibility with senior management.[/46] That anxiety turned out to be largely unfounded. The quality of my output improved because the office I had been working in was genuinely too noisy for the level of concentration my work demands. [50]I now go into the office one day a week purely for face-to-face relationship building, and I think that balance works far better than either extreme.[/50] My biggest concern about full-time remote work had been isolation, but weekly contact combined with active effort to stay connected has proved sufficient.

questions.question: expresses a strong preference for working from home that remains as positive as ever?
questions.explanation: Laura still feels a thrill of satisfaction each morning after six years of remote work and says the freedom to structure her own day would be hard to give up.

questions.question: was uncertain whether remote work would suit their personality?
questions.explanation: Patrick assumed he would struggle with remote working because he had always seen himself as a people person who needed contact with others.

questions.question: realised that a poor physical setup at home was having a negative effect on their well-being?
questions.explanation: Yuki worked from the kitchen table for two years before accepting that her home setup was making her miserable rather than flexible.

questions.question: was worried that working remotely would damage their professional reputation?
questions.explanation: Greg spent the first three months convinced his career would suffer from reduced visibility with senior management.

questions.question: describes a physical change to their home environment that significantly improved both their efficiency and their mood?
questions.explanation: Laura created a dedicated home office with a door that stays shut during work hours, which made an enormous difference to productivity and stress.

questions.question: noticed an unexpected benefit for certain colleagues when switching to remote meetings?
questions.explanation: Patrick was surprised to find that some team members who rarely spoke in the office became the most engaged voices in remote video calls.

questions.question: now actively promotes advice on remote working to others in their field?
questions.explanation: Yuki has become evangelical about the importance of a dedicated workspace and talks about it constantly in her professional network.

questions.question: describes a hybrid arrangement that they believe is more effective than working solely from home or the office?
questions.explanation: Greg goes into the office one day a week for relationship building and thinks that balance works far better than either extreme.

questions.question: identifies a specific aspect of office life that remote working cannot replicate?
questions.explanation: Laura says the one thing she genuinely misses about office life is the unplanned corridor conversations that led to her best ideas.

questions.question: mentions a particular task related to new employees that remains difficult in a remote context?
questions.explanation: Patrick says onboarding new staff remotely is still challenging because building trust takes far longer without a shared physical space.
